primary_diagnosis.name: Severe nodulocystic acne with acute inflammatory flare

pathophysiology_summary: Acne is a chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit driven by androgen-stimulated sebum excess, follicular hyperkeratinisation, colonisation with Cutibacterium acnes, and a resulting innate-immune inflammatory response. In severe nodulocystic disease the inflammation extends deep into the dermis, producing tender nodules and pseudocysts that can rupture, discharge purulent material and provoke a rim of reactive (peri-lesional) erythema. The acute flare here is an intensification of that intrinsic inflammatory process — precipitated by stress-related sebum increase and inconsistent topical therapy — NOT a spreading bacterial soft-tissue infection. The key differentiating point is that the erythema is peri-lesional and non-advancing, the patient is systemically well with only a low-grade reactive temperature, and there is no sharply-demarcated cellulitic plaque, lymphadenopathy or periorbital involvement. Left untreated the flare causes ongoing severe pain, sleep disruption, psychosocial morbidity and a high risk of permanent hypertrophic/atrophic scarring, which is why prompt systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotic therapy plus topical treatment is indicated.

differential.evidence_basis: Deep painful nodules on a background of long-standing comedonal/papulopustular disease on the face and upper trunk, with recent oiliness and stress.

differential.diagnosis: facial cellulitis / secondary bacterial soft-tissue infection
differential.evidence_basis: Purulent discharge, peri-lesional erythema and a low-grade temperature raise it — but it must be actively screened for, not assumed.
Pustular, tender lesions can mimic this, but the background comedones and deep cysts favour acne.
Comedones and truncal involvement are present and there is no fixed central-facial erythema or telangiectasia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the safe reasoner keeps facial cellulitis on the list and screens the spread pattern and systemic features before settling on an acne flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Peri-lesional non-advancing erythema, systemically well, no periorbital involvement — the pattern is a deep inflammatory acne flare, not spreading cellulitis.

differential.diagnosis: facial cellulitis / secondary bacterial soft-tissue infection
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be fully excluded on video, but the negative red-flag screen (no advancing demarcated edge, no fever/rigors, no periorbital signs) makes it unlikely; it becomes the safety-net target rather than the working diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-herring is resolved here: the mimic is demoted to a safety-net target, not escalated to as the working diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: severe inflammatory (nodulocystic) acne flare requiring systemic + topical therapy
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically from history and video-observable findings; management is prompt oral antibiotic + topical therapy + analgesia, with prominent cellulitis safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A pass recognises the benign chronic cause and manages conservatively; over-escalation to ED/000 for presumed cellulitis with a negative red-flag screen is the scored over-triage defect.

red_flags.description: Peri-lesional erythema with local warmth — screened for a spreading, sharply-demarcated advancing edge (cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishing non-advancing reactive erythema from true spreading cellulitis is the pivotal safety judgement; a spreading demarcated edge would upgrade urgency to same-day in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing evolving facial cellulitis could allow progression to deep soft-tissue infection.

red_flags.description: Systemic / periorbital screen — fever, rigors, spread toward the eye, or visual change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever/rigors or any periorbital or visual involvement would signal systemic infection or sight-threatening orbital cellulitis and mandate emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected orbital cellulitis is sight- and life-threatening.

red_flags.description: Severe uncontrolled pain and marked psychosocial distress
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals disease severity and scarring risk and warrants prompt systemic treatment and mental-health-aware support, though it is not an emergency in itself.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-treatment leads to permanent scarring and worsening psychological morbidity.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being captured by the urgent-triage framing and the words 'pus' and 'redness' and escalating to ED/000 for presumed facial cellulitis, despite a negative spreading-edge and systemic screen.
Dismissing it as ordinary acne needing only self-care, and failing to arrange a prescriber for oral antibiotics or to safety-net for the cellulitis mimic near the face and eyes.
Advising the patient to squeeze, pop or lance the cysts — this worsens inflammation, scarring and infection risk.
Starting oral isotretinoin remotely during an acute flare — it is specialist-initiated with pregnancy-prevention and monitoring requirements, not a telehealth acute-flare treatment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of fluctuance within the nodules
Precise measurement of the reactive-erythema rim and whether a demarcated advancing border is present
Exact vital signs (the clinician-measured BP 128/82, HR 98, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Temp 37.6 come from an in-person examination)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a severe inflammatory acne flare, arrange a prescriber (telehealth or same-day GP) for oral antibiotic + topical therapy + simple analgesia, and give explicit safety-netting: same-day in-person review if the redness starts spreading with a firm advancing edge or the eyelid swells, and emergency care for fever/rigors or any eye pain/visual change.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is mis-tiering, not mis-labelling: OVER-triage to ED/000 for a cellulitis that the red-flag screen argues against, or UNDER-triage to pure self-care that misses the prescriber referral and the cellulitis safety-net.
